nuggets.summary: Early in his career as an investment banker, Levitan asked Howard Schultz why Schultz seemed happy and aligned with his work while Levitan felt stuck jetting between deals. Schultz replied that Levitan "hadn't put himself in a position to win" — a line Levitan says reframed his thinking for the next twenty years. The insight is that "winning" isn't universal; you first have to define what winning means for you personally, then check whether your current business model or career path can actually deliver it, independent of how hard you work or how talented you are. Levitan realized his personal definition of winning was mismatched with the investment-banking business model itself, not with his effort, and it took him until age 40 to act on it by quitting and moving to Seattle.
nuggets.quote: he said something that I will never forget, which was, you haven't put yourself in a position to win... I found myself kind of in a mismatch between my own personal business model and being an investment banker.

nuggets.summary: Levitan recounts that in his first meeting with Howard Schultz in 1991, Schultz told him "there are no mensches in investment banking" — using the Yiddish word for someone who is fundamentally decent — as a direct challenge rather than the usual relationship-building small talk. The counterintuitive part is that this worked as a business-development approach precisely because it broke the norm: instead of selling or flattering, Schultz assumed Levitan would understand the word and be honest about the industry's reputation, and it stuck with Levitan for over 20 years, eventually shaping Maveron's own "no assholes, family orientation" culture. The lesson for founders and dealmakers is that blunt, values-based directness in a first meeting can be more memorable and relationship-building than conventional pitch/sell behavior.
nuggets.quote: he looked me in the eye, and he said, there are no mensches in investment banking... it was really fascinating that he kind of, in my mind, as an investment banker, put a bullseye on his forehead by saying, be a mensch, and you'll get my business.

nuggets.summary: Levitan describes Groupon as the investment that best illustrates the gap between market hype and reality — Maveron invested in late 2010 after Levitan says roughly 15 people congratulated him on "getting into Groupon," which in hindsight was itself a warning sign. His rule of thumb, learned from this experience, is that when something is being universally praised as an obviously great deal, that consensus enthusiasm is itself a red flag, even though in this specific case the company eventually recovered and the stock did well. The broader, actionable point is to treat unanimous external excitement about a deal as a signal to slow down and scrutinize rather than a confirmation of quality.
nuggets.quote: I probably met 15 people who said to me, Oh, you've invested in a Groupon? And, you know, normally, when it's too good to be true, it probably is too good to be true.

nuggets.summary: Levitan pushes back on the assumption — common among ambitious professionals — that everyone should aspire to lead or found a company, calling it a "societal pressure" he no longer believes is universally valid, something he says he learned the hard way as CEO of Maveron. He separately notes that startups have become "a little too popular lately," partly fueled by ventures like General Assembly that make entrepreneurship education broadly accessible, and warns that for some people this creates "a trendiness" pursued for its own sake rather than out of real alignment with what they want. The actionable takeaway: before starting or leading a company, deliberately check whether leadership/founding is actually the right fit for your own goals and risk tolerance, rather than defaulting to it because it's currently high-status.
nuggets.quote: I'm not sure that we have such a societal pressure that people have to lead things... for some people, I think there is a trendiness to it that is unfortunate.

nuggets.summary: Asked for three things not to do in a pitch to Maveron, Levitan gives specific, actionable red flags rather than generic advice: presenting a business case built on brand and conviction with no supporting data, and citing personal accomplishments ("I did this, I did that") as a substitute for demonstrating the ability to actually build and lead a team. He frames the underlying standard as needing "an incredible balance between data and heart," meaning founders should show hard evidence and team-building capability together, not lean on one to compensate for the absence of the other.
nuggets.quote: Without any data, don't act like you know this is going to work. Talk to me just about brand and not about facts... Say, I did this, I did that, and prove to me that you can't build a team.

nuggets.summary: Levitan lays out the three specific criteria Maveron's partners use every Monday to decide which deals to pursue: exceptional people (explicitly "the one-tenth of 1%," not just the top 1%), a differentiated, enduring consumer product orientation, and a genuinely large addressable market — because, as he states plainly, venture returns are driven by grand-slam outcomes, not a doubles-and-triples business. He adds a self-check the partnership uses to catch groupthink: unanimous internal enthusiasm about a deal is treated as a warning sign rather than validation, even inside their own investment committee.
nuggets.quote: we're not looking for the 1%. We're looking for the one-tenth of 1% of the person and the team... If it's so obvious, it's probably wrong... If everybody bets, it's probably the wrong bet.

nuggets.summary: Maveron's investment committee deliberately splits judgment by individual partner strength rather than voting as a generalist group — for example, one partner with deep operating experience hiring and firing is the one whose read on whether a founder is an "A entrepreneur" carries the most weight, even though the whole team weighs in. The non-obvious point is that the person who first identifies a promising market or spots the opportunity isn't necessarily the best person to judge how strong the entrepreneur is — those are separate skills — so the firm assigns decision weight by demonstrated strength in each specific judgment area instead of treating every partner's opinion as equally valid on every dimension.
nuggets.quote: the person that might have the insight and vision to say, this is a great space, might not be the best person to really drill down and find out how great an entrepreneur and an executor he or she is... we've learned to trust each other's strengths and hopefully play to those strengths.

nuggets.summary: Levitan cites concrete market-sizing numbers behind Maveron's Trupanion (pet health insurance) investment: Americans spend an estimated $51 billion per year on pets, roughly 25% of pets in the UK are insured, versus only about 2% in the US at the time — a gap of over 10x between a comparable developed market and the US. This kind of stat is a template for how Maveron sizes opportunity: not just "is this a big market" in the abstract, but finding a specific cross-market penetration gap that implies a large amount of unrealized demand.
nuggets.quote: how much do you think Americans spend on pets every year? 51 billion... in the U.K., what percentage of the pets do you think are insured?... 25%. In the U.S., what percentage? Two.

nuggets.summary: When evaluating the Trupanion founder before investing, Levitan treated two specific, unscripted behaviors as strong signals of founder conviction rather than relying on the pitch itself: the founder had hired his own father as an employee (a costly, unusual personal commitment), and when Levitan suggested the founder relocate to Seattle to make the partnership work, the founder called back the next day saying he'd already discussed it with his wife and would move if the deal closed. Levitan treats this kind of unprompted, high-cost personal action — hiring family, being willing to uproot your life overnight — as more diagnostic of whether a founder will persevere through inevitable setbacks than anything said in a pitch meeting.
nuggets.quote: Why don't you move from Bowen Island to Seattle if you think we can add a lot of value? Next day, he calls me up and he says, I spoke to my wife. We'll move if you guys do the deal. You know, again, really interesting data point that says to me, this guy is serious.

nuggets.summary: Levitan calls Maveron's first investment — eBay in June 1998, which IPO'd that September and then kept climbing through his honeymoon — "both a blessing and a curse." The non-obvious lesson is that an extremely easy, extremely early win can be actively harmful to a new investor's development, because it creates false confidence without teaching the harder lessons about risk that come from being knocked down by a bad outcome; he says he doesn't think he's capable of really learning a risk lesson without that pain. The takeaway for anyone early in a high-variance career is to be suspicious of an unusually smooth first success rather than assuming it reflects real skill.
nuggets.quote: eBay was both a blessing and a curse... you can say it's better to be lucky than smart, but unless you get really kicked in the you-know-whats, I don't think I'm capable of learning the lesson.

nuggets.summary: Levitan argues against treating VC/early-stage capital as fungible or purely financial, contrasting it with his view of how later-stage funding may evolve toward companies mostly just needing "fuel to grow" rather than another person at the boardroom table. Early-stage money, he says, "comes with an attitude" — you're bringing on people, relationships, and expectations, not just a check — so founders should scrutinize investors' fit and character with the same seriousness as a permanent hire, not evaluate term sheets on price alone. This directly counters the common founder instinct to treat a seed round as primarily a financing decision.
nuggets.quote: I caution people against looking at early-stage money as fungible. It's not. And early-stage money comes with an attitude. And make sure that the people on your bus are people you want on the bus.

nuggets.summary: Levitan describes the Decide.com deal (sold to eBay) as an example of a founder, Mike Fridgen, actively managing multiple investors (Madrona in the A round, Maveron in the B, Vulcan in the C) into a collaborative rather than competitive dynamic — including asking to sign the term sheet in person and opening that meeting by asking Levitan directly what it would take for this to be one of his best investments. The concrete, transferable move is that a founder can deliberately set the tone of an investor relationship at the moment of signing, using a small ritual to establish psychological investment from the investor before the money even moves — Levitan says it visibly carried through to how the whole board behaved afterward.
nuggets.quote: he says, 'Can I come over to your office? I'd like to sign the term sheet with you in person.'... when he signs the term sheet, he says, 'What would this be to really be one of your best investments? Because that's where I'd like to start.'

nuggets.summary: Levitan identifies "ignoring the data" as the worst recurring failure he sees in boardrooms, and explains the specific mechanism: teams stay committed to their original investment thesis a year or two in, telling themselves they'll work harder or broaden the product rather than confronting evidence the thesis isn't working, because pivoting is emotionally difficult for both the CEO and the board. He also names a related pattern — the "800-pound gorilla under the table," a problem everyone privately sees but no one raises — and argues it's specifically the CEO's job to build a boardroom culture transparent enough that this doesn't happen, since which style works (unilateral decisive CEOs vs. ones who need group iteration) varies by person.
nuggets.quote: ignoring the data in certain circumstances is the worst sin... in a lot of boardrooms, there's the 800-pound gorilla that's under the table that no one has the guts to talk about.

nuggets.summary: Levitan directly challenges the "Silicon Valley vs. Seattle" framing as "nonsense," arguing Seattle is actually an easier place to build a company because of easier hiring and higher employee loyalty, and that its relative obscurity in the tech press is protective rather than a disadvantage: media coverage that lines a startup up against several others doing the same thing invites copying and competitive scrutiny that a company operating below the radar avoids. As evidence, he cites Zulily, which operated in relative obscurity from its founding in Maveron's own office in November 2009 until Andreessen Horowitz's investment made it visible in 2012 — roughly three years of low-profile execution before broader awareness.
nuggets.quote: this Valley Seattle thing, I think, is nonsense... I think the lack of scrutiny, Pando Daily and Crunchbase talking about the five startups that are doing the same thing as you increases your likelihood of success... they started in our office in November of 2009. And really until Andreessen Horowitz invested in them in 2012. No one knew about them.

nuggets.summary: Levitan states flatly that capital is not the constraint in venture — "the shortage is great entrepreneurs" — and that there is too much capital chasing too few great entrepreneurs attacking big ideas even in a reduced funding environment. This runs against the common founder complaint that the money isn't there; Levitan's view is the opposite bottleneck: for a founder trying to raise, the strategic question isn't "how do I find capital" but "how do I position myself as one of the genuinely scarce great entrepreneurs," since capital supply already exceeds that pool.
nuggets.quote: The world doesn't need N+1 VCs. The shortage is great entrepreneurs. There's too much, even in today's reduced environment, there's still too much capital chasing too few great entrepreneurs attacking big ideas.

nuggets.summary: Levitan admits Maveron tried investing outside its consumer-only focus into enterprise deals and explicitly says "we sucked at it," tying the failure to a structural cause: as former investment bankers and consumer-focused operators, neither he nor his team had the operating background to actually help enterprise founders build their companies, whereas evaluating and adding value to consumer businesses played to their actual experience. The generalizable lesson is that a firm's (or individual's) willingness to expand outside its edge of demonstrated ability is itself a common, avoidable investing mistake, not just a fluke deal that didn't work.
nuggets.quote: the reality is we dabbled in enterprise and we sucked at it... what do you do better than other people? And how does that concentration work in your favor?

nuggets.summary: Levitan says the standard career narrative that hard work alone determines success is false, illustrated by a story he heard at his business-school reunion about a man whose housekeeper "works really hard, no one harder in his life than her," yet has not seen financial rewards proportional to that effort. The actionable reframe he draws from it is a two-part test — figure out what you actually like doing and figure out what you're genuinely best at, then pursue the overlap — rather than assuming that sheer effort, independent of fit and talent, is what produces outsized outcomes.
nuggets.quote: hard work isn't the determinant of success. He said, you know, his maid works really hard. No one harder in his life than her. And, you know, I mean, hasn't reaped the financial rewards... Figure out what you like and figure out what you do best and go do it.

nuggets.summary: Maveron deliberately shifted to seed-stage investing after 2009 not because of industry fashion but a specific capital-efficiency calculation: they observed that Series B/C rounds had become too expensive to compete in against larger funds, while it had become far cheaper to prove out consumer traction at the seed stage, letting them put less capital to work yet get meaningful stakes. The strategic reframe was to build "a pipeline of entrepreneurs, not just a pipeline of deals" — investing in relationships with founders earlier so the firm earns the right to participate in later, pricier rounds rather than trying to buy into them competitively.
nuggets.quote: we said it takes far less capital ever to document consumer acceptance and traction. So we could be putting less capital to work yet getting meaningful stakes... we decided to move earlier and frankly create a pipeline of entrepreneurs, not just a pipeline of deals.

nuggets.summary: For early, small seed checks, Maveron applies an explicit internal standard: writing a $100,000 seed check obligates them to work hard enough — via connectivity, help, and reporting cadence — to "earn the right" to lead the company's next (Series A) round, rather than treating the seed as a low-effort placeholder investment. Levitan acknowledges this doesn't always work cleanly and that they've sometimes not evaluated the follow-on round as objectively as a new investor would, meaning the same relationship meant to earn trust can also introduce bias into a later funding decision.
nuggets.quote: in doing the seed, we want to earn the right to do your A. And so that means that we need enough connectivity and enough help that they feel we've earned the right to do the A... occasionally we haven't looked at the situation as objectively as a new investor. And that's painful.
